title: Decision-Making Style Diagnostic
displayTitle: How You Decide With (And Without) AI
tagline: A four-minute diagnostic on the pattern behind your last hundred decisions — and how AI is reshaping it.

children.text: Eight short decision scenarios. For each, four ways a senior leader might actually approach it. Pick the one closest to what you'd do — not the one that sounds right.

After the scenarios, three short questions about how you use AI in your decisions. The result is a placement on a two-by-two of decision-making style, plus a read on how AI is interacting with your default.

questions.prompt: You're hiring for a senior role. You've interviewed five strong candidates. You need to decide.

options.label: Build a weighted scoring rubric across all criteria. Pick whoever scores highest.
Identify the must-haves. Pick the first candidate who clears all of them.
Picture each one in the role for thirty seconds. Pick whoever feels right. Refine the gut.
Trust your top-of-mind person from the start. Move on.

questions.prompt: Your team needs to commit to one major tooling decision — significant cost, significant lock-in, year-long evaluation cycle.

options.label: Spreadsheet of features by costs by reviews. Score each. Decide.
Pick the two most credible in your network. Pilot one. If it works, commit.
Look at the contenders. Sense which one fits how your team actually works. Pressure-test that.
The first one that looked right. Get on with it.

questions.prompt: Two viable strategies for the next year. Both have merit. Both have risks.

options.label: Model both. Run the numbers. Compare expected value.
List the dealbreakers. Whichever doesn't trigger one, go.
Sit with each. Notice which one you keep coming back to. Pressure-test that.
You already know which one. Make it work.

questions.prompt: A function in your remit could be reshaped: keep as-is, augment with AI tooling, or restructure with significantly fewer people. Real consequences either way.

options.label: Build a thorough business case across all three options. Decide on the merits.
Identify the option that keeps the most strategic optionality. Go with that.
Look at the people involved. Imagine each scenario playing out. Decide on the version you can defend.
You already sense which direction this is going. Lead it.

questions.prompt: You're being approached about a different role — internally or externally. The numbers look reasonable. Something feels off.

options.label: Decision matrix: pros, cons, weighted. See where the math lands.
Identify the one or two factors that matter most. Decide on those.
Trust the "feels off" signal. Investigate what it is before deciding.
The off feeling is the answer. Decline.

questions.prompt: A multi-year initiative is on the table. Real budget, real political capital.

options.label: Full business case. NPV. Sensitivity analysis. Decide on the numbers.
Stage-gate it. Commit to phase one. Reassess.
Look at who's championing it. If you'd back them on something else, back this.
You can already tell whether it's a yes. Commit.

questions.prompt: Two senior people on your team disagree on a substantive call. They're both credible. You have to break the tie.

options.label: Surface every argument, weight them, decide on the merits.
Identify which decision is more reversible if wrong. Go with that.
Read what's underneath the disagreement, including what neither of them is saying. Decide accordingly.
Pick the one whose instincts have been right more often. Defend the call.

questions.prompt: An initiative you backed isn't working as planned. You need to decide whether to push through or change course.

options.label: Run the data. Set a threshold. If you're below it, pivot.
Give it a defined window. If no improvement by date X, pivot.
Notice when you're rationalising vs. when you're committed. Decide from there.
You probably already know. Stop debating it.

calibrationQuestions.prompt: When you face a substantive decision, how often do you consult an AI tool as part of your process?

options.label: Never
Occasionally
Often
Almost always

calibrationQuestions.prompt: When the AI's output disagrees with your instinct, how often do you go with the AI?

options.label: Never
Occasionally
Often
Almost always

calibrationQuestions.prompt: How confident are you that you'd notice if the AI's reasoning was subtly wrong?

options.label: Not confident
Somewhat confident
Mostly confident
Very confident

children.text: You want the perfect answer, and your gut won't let you settle for less. Strong pattern recognition is your edge. The risk is the hardest of the four to defend, vulnerable to bias dressed as intuition, and prone to rejecting good options waiting for the perfect one.

You decide fast, trust yourself, and move on. Speed and momentum are your edge — you start things others are still debating. The risk is making consequential calls before gathering information, and being vulnerable to confident wrong people because you decide before checking.

You want the best answer, and you'll do the work to find it. Rigour is your edge. The risk is decision paralysis when the data is incomplete (and it always is), over-investment in decisions whose stakes don't justify it, and underweighting your own pattern-recognition because it doesn't show up on the spreadsheet.

You want a good-enough answer, found through clear thinking. Efficiency and decisiveness are your edge — you're the person who unsticks groups. The risk is moving on before stress-testing whether "good enough" was right, mistaking speed for clarity, and instincting your way out of the genuinely high-stakes call that warranted exhaustive analysis.

Your pattern recognition is doing real work for you, but it's also doing it invisibly — which means you can't easily inspect it or defend it. Your best calls are exceptional. Your worst ones share a quality: at the time you couldn't have explained why you were sure, and afterwards you couldn't reconstruct what you were certain of. The question to sit with: when was the last time you trusted your gut and were clearly, demonstrably wrong? What did you learn?

You make most of your decisions before consciously deciding. For the vast majority of small and medium calls, this is efficient and probably correct. The pattern matters most on the one or two consequential calls a year where additional information would have changed the outcome — those are the decisions your style is least equipped to identify in advance. The question to sit with: which of your decisions over the last year deserved more time than you gave them?

Your reasoning is hard to attack and your decisions are defensible. Both of those have a cost. The cost is the decisions you're still analysing when the window closes, and the decisions whose stakes never deserved the depth you brought. The question to sit with: which of your last five decisions actually needed the analysis you gave them?

You're the person who unsticks groups. You see the must-haves, identify the option that clears them, and move. This is genuine leadership work. The watch-out is your blind spot for the decision that warranted exhaustive analysis but felt like a normal one — your style finds an answer fast, which means you may have moved on from a call that needed another forty hours of work. The question to sit with: what's the difference between a decision you've made and a decision you've moved on from?

You're trusting your pattern recognition without an external check. For most of your career that's been fine — you've built the patterns. The current question is whether the territory has shifted enough that some of your patterns are no longer valid. AI won't replace your intuition, but it can show you what your intuition is missing. The work isn't using AI to decide — it's using AI to surface what you'd otherwise not consider.

You're using AI as a calibration tool rather than a substitute, which is the right move for your style. The watch-out: when the AI disagrees with your gut, you have to actually update sometimes, not just note the disagreement and proceed. Without that, AI becomes a confirmation device — which is worse than not using it at all, because it gives the confidence of having checked without the cost of having genuinely checked.

The most interesting combination in this assessment. Strong intuition plus heavy AI use can go two ways. Either AI is sharpening your pattern recognition by giving you more data and faster comparisons, or it's slowly eroding your confidence in your own judgement by offering a more articulate-sounding alternative on every call. Which is happening is worth knowing. Test: when AI agrees with you, do you feel more or less certain than before you asked? More is fine. Less is a warning.

You decide fast and don't consult external sources, including AI. This works for high-volume small decisions — which is most of your day. It's high-risk for the few large decisions in your year where additional information would have changed the call. The discipline is identifying which decisions belong in which category. Hint: if you find yourself thinking "this is a quick one" about a multi-year commitment, your style is fooling you.

You're using AI as a sanity check rather than a decision-maker, which is the right move for your style. The work is using it on the right decisions — your habit is to decide before checking, so the discipline is pausing long enough to consult on the calls that warrant it. Worth building a personal trigger: a category of decision where you commit, in advance, to running the AI check before you commit.

The riskiest combination in this assessment. You decide quickly and intuitively, and you're now supplementing those decisions with a tool that produces confident-sounding output instantly. The two together can produce decisions that feel well-supported because the AI agreed, when really the AI agreed because you described the question in a way that constrained its answer. Worth slowing down on the high-stakes ones — and worth examining how you're framing your prompts on the rest.

You're doing the rigorous work but not using a tool that genuinely accelerates the rigorous work. The question worth holding: is this discipline, or habit? If it's discipline — you've evaluated AI and decided the marginal value isn't there for your decisions — fair. If it's habit, the cost is real and growing as the tools get better. Test: when did you last use an AI tool seriously on a real decision, not as a demo?

You're using AI to augment your analytical process, which is the obvious right move for your style. The risk in your profile is subtle: AI tools tend to confirm the structure of your analysis, which means they also confirm your blind spots. The specific discipline worth building: periodically ask the AI to make the opposite case to your own conclusion. Not because you'll be persuaded, but because the gaps in its argument will tell you where the gaps in yours are.

Your style and AI are well-matched on the surface — both want comprehensive analysis. The specific risk is subtle: you may be outsourcing the parts of the analysis that would have produced original insight to a tool that produces consensus output. The work is reserving the highest-stakes analysis for your own head, not the model's. AI is excellent at synthesising the median view. Your value as a senior leader is being above the median view. Don't let the tool flatten that.

You're already fast and decisive — AI tools probably won't make you much faster, but they would catch the occasional thing you'd otherwise miss. The question is whether the friction of consulting one outweighs the value of the catch. For most decisions, probably yes. For the high-stakes ones, probably no. The discipline is recognising which is which in advance, not in hindsight.

Probably the cleanest fit of style and AI use in this assessment. You use it where it helps and trust yourself where it doesn't. The only watch-out: this combination is so frictionless that it can lead to decisions that feel rigorous because the AI agreed, when actually the AI just confirmed the satisficing instinct you already had. Periodically check: am I using AI to test my thinking, or to validate it?

You're an instinctively fast decider relying heavily on a tool with a strong bias toward producing confident-sounding output. The risk profile here is worth taking seriously: you're optimised for speed, and AI is now amplifying that speed without necessarily improving the underlying judgement. Worth slowing down on a defined category of decision — the multi-year, hard-to-reverse ones — and using AI to widen the option set you considered, not to compress the deliberation.

Your answers indicate heavy AI use combined with low confidence that you'd catch errors in the AI's reasoning. This combination has a specific risk profile regardless of your underlying decision style, and it's worth addressing before reading the style-specific result below. The practical move: pick a recent significant decision where you used AI heavily and reconstruct what you'd have decided without it. The exercise is uncomfortable; it's also where the work is.

Your answers indicate you don't consult AI tools on substantive decisions. This may be principled or it may be inertial — only you can tell which. If it's principled, the result below stands. If it's inertial, the read on your style is incomplete: you may be operating with patterns that haven't been tested against a tool that's increasingly part of how your peers decide. Worth one experiment: pick the next non-trivial decision and run it through an AI tool, not to follow its answer but to see whether the way it framed the question changes how you frame it yourself.
Want your full diagnostic — decisions your style handles well, decisions it handles badly, and three practical tactics for working with the other three styles plus AI?
Or — if this surfaced something worth talking through — I do a free thirty-minute conversation specifically for this kind of reflection. No agenda, no upsell.

seoTitle: How You Decide With (And Without) AI — fab.partners
seoDescription: A four-minute diagnostic on the pattern behind your last hundred decisions. Four styles, three AI bands, twelve profiles.
